questions.question: The `pwd` command prints the name of the current directory.
questions.explanation: Correct. pwd command shows the name of the current directory.
questions.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory

questions.question: The `basename` command prints the directory part of a path.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. basename command strips any leading directory components from a name.
questions.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name

questions.question: If a string contains no slash, `dirname` prints `.`.
questions.explanation: Correct. If the input string contains no slash, dirname prints '.'.
questions.source_section: 18.2 dirname: Strip last file name component

questions.question: The `true` command is used as a placeholder where an unsuccessful command is needed.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. true command exits with status 0, meaning success.
questions.source_section: 16.2 true: Do nothing, successfully

questions.question: The `false` command returns exit status 1, meaning failure.
questions.explanation: Correct. false command exits with status 1, meaning failure.
questions.source_section: 16.1 false: Do nothing, unsuccessfully

questions.question: The `dirname` command prints only the final slash-delimited component of a path.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. dirname command shows all but the final slash-delimited component of each name.
questions.source_section: 18.2 dirname: Strip last file name component

questions.question: With `pwd --logical`, the command always resolves every symbolic link in the printed path.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. With --logical, pwd produces the contents of PWD if PWD is an absolute name of the current directory with no '.' or '..' components.
questions.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory

questions.question: With `pwd --physical`, the printed path can still contain symbolic links.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. With --physical, pwd shows a fully resolved name for the current directory with no symbolic links in the printed path.
questions.source_section: 19.1 pwd: Print working directory

questions.question: If `basename` is given a suffix that matches the end of the name, it can remove that suffix.
questions.explanation: Correct. If basename is given a suffix identical to the end of the name, that suffix is removed.
questions.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name

questions.question: The `basename --zero` option ends each line with a newline character.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. basename --zero option outputs a zero byte at the end of each line rather than a newline.
questions.source_section: 18.1 basename: Strip directory and suffix from a file name

questions.question: The `echo` command writes strings separated by spaces and adds a newline after the last one.
questions.explanation: Correct. echo command writes each given string to standard output with a space between strings and a newline after the last one.
questions.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text

questions.question: The `echo -n` option forces a trailing newline to be printed.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. echo -n option does not output the trailing newline.
questions.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text

questions.question: The `echo -e` option enables interpretation of backslash escapes.
questions.explanation: Correct. echo -e option enables interpretation of backslash-escaped characters in each string.
questions.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text

questions.question: The `echo -E` option enables interpretation of backslash escapes.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. echo -E option disables interpretation of backslash escapes in each string.
questions.source_section: 15.1 echo: Print a line of text

questions.question: The `printf` command does formatted printing of text.
questions.explanation: Correct. printf command does formatted printing of text.
questions.source_section: 15.2 printf: Format and print data

questions.question: The `printf` command ignores both `%` directives and backslash escapes in its format string.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. printf command shows the format string, interpreting percent directives and backslash escapes to format numeric and string arguments.
questions.source_section: 15.2 printf: Format and print data

questions.question: The `yes` command repeats its command line arguments, separated by spaces and followed by a newline, until it is killed.
questions.explanation: Correct. yes command shows its command line arguments, separated by spaces and followed by a newline, forever until it is killed.
questions.source_section: 15.3 yes: Print a string until interrupted

questions.question: If `yes` is run with no arguments, it repeatedly prints `n`.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. If yes is invoked with no arguments, it prints 'y' followed by a newline forever until killed.
questions.source_section: 15.3 yes: Print a string until interrupted

questions.question: The `printenv` command prints environment variable values.
questions.explanation: Correct. printenv command shows environment variable values.
questions.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables

questions.question: If no variable is specified, `printenv` prints only one environment variable.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. If no variables are specified, printenv prints the value of every environment variable.
questions.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables

questions.question: The `printenv --null` option ends each line with a NUL byte instead of a newline.
questions.explanation: Correct. printenv --null option outputs a zero byte at the end of each line rather than a newline.
questions.source_section: 19.3 printenv: Print all or some environment variables

questions.question: The `tty` command prints the file name of the terminal connected to standard output.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. tty command shows the file name of the terminal connected to standard input.
questions.source_section: 19.4 tty: Print file name of terminal on standard input

questions.question: If standard input is not a terminal, `tty` prints `not a tty`.
questions.explanation: Correct. If standard input is not a terminal, tty prints 'not a tty'.
questions.source_section: 19.4 tty: Print file name of terminal on standard input

questions.question: The `logname` command prints the calling user's name from a system-maintained file.
questions.explanation: Correct. logname command shows the calling user's name as found in a system-maintained file.
questions.source_section: 20.2 logname: Print current login name

questions.question: If there is no entry for the calling process, `logname` exits with status 0.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. If there is no entry for the calling process, logname exits with status 1.
questions.source_section: 20.2 logname: Print current login name

questions.question: The `whoami` command prints the user name associated with the current effective user ID.
questions.explanation: Correct. whoami command shows the user name associated with the current effective user ID.
questions.source_section: 20.3 whoami: Print effective user name

questions.question: The `whoami` command is equivalent to `id -gn`.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. whoami command has the same effect as the command id -un.
questions.source_section: 20.3 whoami: Print effective user name

questions.question: The `date` command displays the date and time.
questions.explanation: Correct. date command shows the date and time.
questions.source_section: 21.1 date: Print or set system date and time

questions.question: With `--set`, the `date` command sets the date and time and then exits without displaying it.
questions.explanation: Incorrect. With the --set option, date sets the date and time before displaying it.
questions.source_section: 21.1 date: Print or set system date and time

questions.question: If no options are given, `uname` acts as if `-s` were given.
questions.explanation: Correct. If no options are given, uname acts as if the -s option were given.
questions.source_section: 21.4 uname: Print system information
